title: AI Trust & Safety Digest
tagline: A curated current snapshot of AI trust, safety, and governance, including as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
lead: Today’s signal is split between primary-source disclosures from OpenAI and a cluster of new research on evaluation, explainability, fairness, and accountability. The strongest developments focus on how advanced models are documented and stress-tested, plus how auditors can better attribute failures, assess explanations, and measure harms in high-stakes deployments.

items.title: GPT-5.5 Bio Bug Bounty
items.why_it_matters: It expands external red teaming for bio misuse, testing whether safeguards resist jailbreaks under real incentives.

items.title: GPT-5.5 System Card
items.why_it_matters: System cards are core evidence for model risk, safeguards, evaluation scope, and deployment constraints.

items.title: Rethinking XAI Evaluation: A Human-Centered Audit of Shapley Benchmarks in High-Stakes Settings
items.why_it_matters: It questions whether standard explainability benchmarks reflect human usefulness in consequential decisions.

items.title: How Supply Chain Dependencies Complicate Bias Measurement and Accountability Attribution in AI Hiring Applications
items.why_it_matters: It highlights governance gaps when multiple vendors blur responsibility for bias and compliance.

items.title: Seeing the Whole Elephant: A Benchmark for Failure Attribution in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
items.why_it_matters: It targets a key safety problem: identifying which agent and step caused harmful system behavior.

items.title: Measuring Epistemic Unfairness for Algorithmic Decision-Making
items.why_it_matters: It proposes a broader fairness lens for systems that shape what people can know or contest.

items.title: Inferring Equivalence Classes from Legacy Undocumented Embedded Binaries for ISO 26262-Compliant Testing
items.why_it_matters: It addresses assurance for safety-critical software when documentation is missing but standards still apply.

note: Selected from recent trust-and-safety relevant posts and papers by labs, evaluators, standards bodies, policy organizations, and arXiv, then summarized with GPT-5.4. Includes as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
